coredata.dc:title: Facial preparation strategy of high-efficiency carbon dots-based anti-ultraviolet and anti-blue light shielding film and its application in LED
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wanting
Jin, Xilang
Ma, Xuehao
Huang, Bin
Zhang, Xinyu
Li, Xiao
Si, Qingfa
Chen, Weixing
coredata.dc:description: To minimize the harmful effects of ultraviolet (UV) radiation on the skin and eyes, the development of protective films that block UV rays and high-energy blue light (HEBL) while maintaining a high transmittance rate of visible light has become imperative. In this work, hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (hPEI) and hydroxybenzaldehyde were utilized as precursors to prepare carbon dots (CDs) with maximum absorption wavelengths in both the UV and HEBL bands, as well as excellent tolerance and acid/base resistance. In addition, CDs were incorporated into the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix to form CDs shielding films. The primary mechanism is to utilize CN in CDs to form hydrogen bonds with OH in PVA to fix CDs in PVA. These films achieved maximum UV blocking efficiencies exceeding 98.87 % and HEBL blocking efficiencies over 85.08 %, while the highest visible light transmittance reached 70 %. Furthermore, these films were applied to a light-emitting diode (LED), adjusting the color temperature from cool to warm tones by blocking the 400–470 nm blue light range. This work not only introduces a novel strategy for preparing bifunctional UV absorbers but also paves the way for the advancement of health-oriented lighting solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Dots
UV absorbers
Anti-blue light
Polyvinyl alcohol
LED
Warm white

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of CDs in PVA films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Technology Innovation Leading Program of Shaanxi (No. 2020QFY07-05 ) and the Natural Science Foundation Research Project of Shaanxi Province (No. 2023-JC-YB-087 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthesis and application of CDs.

(a–d) HR-TEM imaging of D-CDs, O-CDs, M-CDs and P-CDs. Inset: particle size distribution (top left) and lattice spacing (bottom left) of CDs; (e) XRD spectra of CDs; (f) FT-IR spectrum of CDs; (g–i) XPS survey, C 1s and N 1s of D-CDs, O-CDs, M-CDs and P-CDs.

(a) digital photographs of CDs upon UV light and daylight; (b) UV absorption spectra of D-CDs, O-CDs, M-CDs and P-CDs at the same concentration; (c–f) UV absorption spectra of D-CDs, O-CDs, M-CDs and P-CDs with increasing concentration.
(a–d) TG of D-CDs, O-CDs, M-CDs and P-CDs; (e–h) Corresponding UV absorption spectra of different CDs under acidic, neutral and alkaline conditions.

(a) Photographs of D-CDs/PVA, O-CDs/PVA, M-CDs/PVA and P-CDs/PVA at different concentrations under natural light; (b–e) UV-absorption photoplethysmograms; (f–i) PL plots of different concentrations of D-CDs/PVA, O-CDs/PVA, M-CDs/PVA and P-CDs/PVA.

(a,b) UV absorption and transmission spectra of 5% CDs/PVA films; (c) Comparison of CDs/PVA blocking UV-A, UV-B and high energy blue light; (d) Photographs of rhodamine B under UV light and graphs of the UV-blocking ability of D-CDs/PVA, O-CDs/PVA, M-CDs/PVA, and P-CDs/PVA. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Possible mechanism for UV and HEBL shielding of PVA films.

(a,b) 370 nm and 460 nm chip spectra covering CDs/PVA films; (c,d) Spectra and corresponding CIE coordinate plots of cool-white LED chips and white LED covered with one or two layers of D-CDs/PVA film.

head.ce:title: Facial preparation strategy of high-efficiency carbon dots-based anti-ultraviolet and anti-blue light shielding film and its application in LED

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Weifang Polygrand Chemical CO., LTD, Weifang Changyi Binhai (Xiaying) Economic Development Zone, Weifang 261300, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Weifang Changyi Binhai (Xiaying) Economic Development Zone

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Weifang Polygrand Chemical CO., LTD, Weifang Changyi Binhai (Xiaying) Economic Development Zone, Weifang 261300, Shandong, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xi’an Technological University, Xi’an 710032, Shaanxi, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To minimize the harmful effects of ultraviolet (UV) radiation on the skin and eyes, the development of protective films that block UV rays and high-energy blue light (HEBL) while maintaining a high transmittance rate of visible light has become imperative. In this work, hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (hPEI) and hydroxybenzaldehyde were utilized as precursors to prepare carbon dots (CDs) with maximum absorption wavelengths in both the UV and HEBL bands, as well as excellent tolerance and acid/base resistance. In addition, CDs were incorporated into the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix to form CDs shielding films. The primary mechanism is to utilize CN in CDs to form hydrogen bonds with OH in PVA to fix CDs in PVA. These films achieved maximum UV blocking efficiencies exceeding 98.87 % and HEBL blocking efficiencies over 85.08 %, while the highest visible light transmittance reached 70 %. Furthermore, these films were applied to a light-emitting diode (LED), adjusting the color temperature from cool to warm tones by blocking the 400–470 nm blue light range. This work not only introduces a novel strategy for preparing bifunctional UV absorbers but also paves the way for the advancement of health-oriented lighting solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Harmful ultraviolet light (UV) is mainly in the UV-A (315–400 nm) and UV-B (280–315 nm) bands [1,2]. UV-A radiation can penetrate the upper dermis, affecting blood vessels and other tissues, resulting in long-term, chronic, and persistent damage, leading to premature aging of the skin [3]. UV-B radiation is mainly absorbed by the epidermis and can cause skin damage, including the development of skin cancer with prolonged exposure [4,5]. In addition to UV radiation, high-energy blue light (HEBL) in the visible light spectrum can also cause significant harm to the human body [6–8]. Most electronic devices we use nowadays, such as smartphones, computers, and televisions, utilize light-emitting diode (LED) backlighting, which can emit blue light [9,10]. Due to its short wavelength and high frequency, blue light can cause discomfort symptoms, including visual fatigue, dry eyes, headaches, and insomnia. Severe cases may even lead to structural changes in the eye or corneal damage [11–13]. Therefore, a new functional material must be prepared as soon as possible to prevent UV radiation and HEBL from causing harm to the human body.

Due to their graphite carbon structure and abundant surface functional groups, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit absorption in both the UV and visible light regions [14]. This property makes them applicable in various fields, including luminescent solar concentrators [15,16], LED technology [17,18], UV blocking [19,20] and other applications [21–24]. Lei Wang et al. [25] synthesized nitrogen-phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dot compounds (NP-CQDs) and incorporated them into a chitosan matrix, with a visible light transmittance of 73.2 %, but only achieving a maximum of 90.2 % UV-A and 96.5 % UV-B blocking. Concurrently, Huazhang Gu et al. [26] prepared Y-CQDs/PVA films employing an alkali-catalyzed molecular fusion approach aimed at filtering short-wavelength blue light. These films were substantiated to confer retinal protection via biological assays. However, visible light transmittance was compromised when Y-CQD content exceeded the 20 wt% threshold. Nonetheless, the challenge of using CDs for both blue light and UV protection is considerable, and research on this topic is limited. Barun Kumar Barman et al. [18] prepared NCDs using citric acid and ethylenediamine, and efficiently incorporated them into cellulose matrices to form NCDs@cel films, which block up to the full range of UV-A, about 95 % of UV-B, but only 50 % of HEBL and 60 % of visible light transparency. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop bifunctional CDs materials that achieve high UV and HEBL blocking efficiency while maintaining excellent visible light transmittance.

Hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (hPEI) has the advantages of dense charge density, easy modification with a large number of amino groups, which possesses high reactivity and can react with the keto carbonyl group to generate CN double bonds with high bond energy to absorb more ultraviolet rays [27–29]. Therefore, four UV-absorbing CDs were prepared by a simple solvothermal method using hPEI and four hydroxybenzaldehyde derivatives as the main precursor in this work. And they were further doped in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to prepare bifunctional CDs films. These films achieve high visible light transmission while blocking up to 98.87 % of UV radiation and 85.08 % of blue light. To validate the applicability of the CDs films, they were applied to blue and white LED lights, resulting in a reduction of HEBL and enabling color temperature adjustment from cool white to warm white.

Hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (MW = 1800), 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde, o-hydroxybenzaldehyde, m-hydroxybenzaldehyde, p-hydroxybenzaldehyde, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and ethanol were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and all chemicals were used without further purification. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of >18.25 MΩ cm was used in the experiment.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, TcnaiG2F20 S-TWIN) was used to characterize the microscopic morphology and dispersion characteristics of CDs. Crystalline analysis of CDs was performed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Bruker D2 PHASER Gen2). The chemical structure of CDs was analyzed with Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) (Bruker VERTEX 70 infrared spectrometer). The refined structure of the CDs was measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (the K- Thermo Scientific Alpha X-ray spectrometer). The absorption and transmission spectra of the samples were collected by the Shimadzu UV-2550 (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples were recorded at room temperature using a HORIBA QuantaMater 8000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Thermal stability analysis of the samples was performed by the TGA/DSC1_* professional synchronous thermal analyzer.

CDs are synthesized from the bottom up by a one-step solvothermal method (Fig. 1 ). 6.0 mL hPEI was dissolved in 60 mL ethanol with 0.6 g 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde, o-hydroxybenzaldehyde, m-hydroxy-benzaldehyde and p-hydroxybenzaldehyde, respectively. The obtained solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene high-pressure reactor and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The solution after the reaction was cooled to room temperature and filtered with a 0.2 μm membrane filter to remove larger aggregates. The solution was subsequently purified using dialysis bags (1000 Da) in ultrapure water for 48 h to remove unreacted small molecules and impurities. Finally, the four resulting solutions were freeze-dried to obtain pure CDs for further characterization. The four obtained CDs were designated as D-CDs, O-CDs, M-CDs, and P-CDs, respectively.

PVA powder (4.0 g) was dissolved in 40 mL water, stirred with a magnetic stirrer at 90 °C to be completely transparent, and cooled to room temperature to obtain a polyvinyl alcohol solution. Then the polyvinyl alcohol solution was divided into four equal portions and poured into four beakers, to which four different CDs solutions were added respectively, and stirred for 1 h. Finally, the liquid was transferred to a petri dish and dried at 80 °C for 24 h to obtain four CDs films: D-CDs/PVA, O-CDs/PVA, M-CDs/PVA and P-CDs/PVA.

To confirm the formation of CDs, Fig. 2 (a–d) presents the HR-TEM images and the size distribution of the prepared CDs. These CDs display a spherical nanoparticle morphology with a lattice spacing of 0.21 to 0.22 nm, consistent with the (100) lattice plane of graphite. This observation demonstrates the high degree of crystallinity of the CDs [19]. The size distributions of CDs were analyzed using Nano Measurer software in the TEM images. The size distribution histograms and Gaussian distribution plots were depicted in the inset of Fig. 2a–d. These results underscore the average sizes of the four CDs in the following sequence: P-CDs (1.76 nm) < O-CDs (2.84 nm) < M-CDs (2.87 nm) < D-CDs (4.20 nm).

The XRD spectra for D-CDs, O-CDs, M-CDs, and P-CDs exhibit broad peaks at angles of 20.3°, 20.8°, 21.35°, and 22.5° (as illustrated in Fig. 2 e). These peaks are associated with the (100) crystal planes of graphitic carbon, providing further evidence for the presence of graphitic structures within the CDs [30]. The FT-IR spectra presented in Fig. 2 f offer additional structural insights and reveal the surface functional groups of the CDs. Both hPEI and the CDs products exhibited amino-stretching vibrational peaks around 3360 cm−1, indicating a high concentration of amino groups. However, the CDs displayed a broader peak at this frequency compared to the original hPEI, along with a redshift, which can be attributed to the incorporation of OH groups into the CDs. Notably, compared to the precursor hPEI, D-CDs, O-CDs, M-CDs, and P-CDs all showed CN bond stretching vibrational peaks generated by the reaction of CO and NH2 around 1669 cm−1 [31–33]. These results indicate that a significant number of amino functional groups may be present on the surface of the CDs, suggesting that the CDs possess excellent hydrophilicity. To confirm these findings, the elemental composition of the CDs was further analyzed using XPS. As depicted in Fig. 2 g, the total XPS spectra of the CDs reveal the presence of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s elemental peaks, situated near 284.86 eV, 400.07 eV, and 531.69 eV respectively, endorsing the successful doping of oxygen into hPEI via hydroxybenzaldehyde [34,35]. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra for the four CDs are presented in Fig. 2 h, all exhibiting CC and CN bonds located proximally at 284.58 eV and 285.08 eV, respectively [36]. The high-resolution XPS diffraction spectra of N 1s for the four CDs are shown in Fig. 2 i, where the CN and CN bonds are located at 398.81 eV and 399.40 eV, indicating that the active amino group is still much more than the CN double bond [37]. The high-resolution XPS spectrogram of O1s showed the presence of only one peak attributed to COH at 532.14 eV ( Fig. S1 ). Based on the results presented above, it can be concluded that abundant amino and hydroxyl groups are present in the structure of the CDs. The presence of these functional groups imparts excellent water solubility to the CDs, facilitating the formation of a homogeneous and stable composite film through hydrogen bonding between the CDs and the surface groups of PVA.

Optical photographs (Fig. 3
a) show that the four CDs can appear as yellowish or colorless transparent solutions in daylight and blue to green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. The absorption spectra of CDs at a concentration of 0.6 g/L are shown in Fig. 3 b. All the CDs absorption spectra show characteristic absorption peaks at 280–295 nm for the formation of the large π-bond conjugation between the carbon core and the surface groups. The absorption peak at 350 nm corresponds to the UV absorption of varying intensities, which can be attributed to the n-π* transitions of the Schiff base bonds formed during the ammonia-formaldehyde reaction [34]. Furthermore, at a wavelength of 470 nm, all four types of CDs exhibited significant absorption, and the magnitude of absorbance intensity was in the order of M-CDs > D-CDs > O-CDs > P-CDs. This suggests that the synthesized CDs possess promising potential for the absorption of HEBL. Combined with the FT-IR and XPS results, this phenomenon may be caused by the synergistic effect of the carbon nuclei, the surface defects and the functional groups, such as OH and CN, on the surface of CDs. These imply that the prepared D-CDs and M-CDs have a stronger ability to absorb UV and HEBL.

The UV–visible absorption spectra of the four CDs with increasing concentration are shown in Fig. 3 c–f. As the content of CDs in aqueous solution increases, although their absorption intensities gradually increase, the positions of the UV absorption peaks remain unchanged, and all of them have two major absorption peaks in the UV band. Importantly, as the content of D-CDs,O-CDs and M-CDs increased, the absorbing ability for HEBL also gradually increased, which can be found from the wavelength of 400 nm-500 nm. This indicates that these CDs can be utilized in the fields of UV and HEBL absorption when the appropriate concentration is chosen to meet market demand.

To learn the stability of the prepared CDs, their thermal stability and UV absorption characteristics under strong acid or alkaline conditions were investigated. Upon detailed analysis of the TG results (Fig. 4
a–d), it was found that D-CDs, O-CDs, M-CDs, and P-CDs all exhibited approximately 10 % thermal weight loss at 100 °C, primarily due to the evaporation of water content in the CDs. Further observations revealed that D-CDs, O-CDs, M-CDs, and P-CDs exhibited significant thermal decomposition at temperatures of 368 °C, 369 °C, 361 °C, and 358 °C, respectively. At 400 °C, they retained 2.36 %, 0.23 %, 11.26 %, and 0.40 % of their original mass. Given these relatively high decomposition temperatures, it is shown that the four CDs possess good thermal stability.

There are so many functional groups on the surface of CDs that the UV absorption characteristics are strongly affected by pH. The UV–visible absorption spectra of different CDs under acidic or alkaline conditions are shown in Fig. 4 e and f. It is evident that compared to pH = 7, CDs maintain good UV absorption stability under acidic conditions, and under alkaline conditions, the UV absorption of CDs red-shifts due to deprotonation, yet still maintains good UV absorption. (In the case of M-CDs, for example, there are two major UV absorption peaks at 278 nm and 354 nm at pH = 1–8, but these peaks are red-shifted to 290 nm and 375 nm at pH = 10–12. It is worth noting that the UV absorption intensity is not significantly affected by acidic or alkaline conditions.) Additionally, in the 400–500 nm range of the HEBL region, all four CDs maintain relatively high absorption intensity, indicating that the absorption in the HEBL region is also minimally influenced by pH. These findings suggest that the four CDs exhibit good thermal stability and resistance to acidic and alkaline conditions, which further ensures their applicability in a wider range of scenarios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs in PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of CDs contents on the UV absorption properties of PVA films were shown in Fig. 5b–e. The pure PVA film showed no significant UV absorption. As the concentration of CDs in the PVA matrix increased from 1 wt% to 5 wt%, the UV absorption properties of the CDs/PVA films also enhanced. Notably, the absorption intensity of D-CDs/PVA and M-CDs/PVA displayed the most significant variations. When the content of D-CDs in PVA was increased from 1 wt% to 5 wt%, the UV absorption intensity at 360 nm increased from 0.30 L·g−1·cm−1 increased to 1.89 L·g−1·cm−1 (Fig. 5 b). Similarly, in M-CDs/PVA the UV absorption intensity at 360 nm increased from 0.35 L·g−1·cm−1 to 1.05 L·g−1·cm−1 (Fig. 5 d). the result further suggests that CDs can enhance the UV absorption capacity of PVA films. The fluorescence spectra of 5 wt% concentration of CDs/PVA are shown in Fig. 5 f–i, respectively. At different excitation wavelengths, the PVA films doped with different CDs showed green to cyan fluorescence, respectively.

To explore the role of CDs-doped PVA films in UV and HEBL screening, the UV absorption and transmission spectra of CDs/PVA films with 5 wt% CDs concentration were measured (Fig. 6
a and b). The results showed that pure PVA films have almost no UV absorption, and is highly transparent (90 %) in both the UV-A and visible regions, and can only block about 18 % of UV-B light (300 nm). Therefore, unmodified films are not suitable for effective UV blocking. In contrast, D-CDs/PVA and M-CDs/PVA films doped with 5 wt% of the corresponding CDs have two distinct absorption peaks in the UV region and near-zero UV-A and UV-B transmittance, with a visible light transparency of 70.5 % and 68.5 %, respectively. D-CDs/PVA and M-CDs/PVA films also possess a large degree of shielding in the HEBL. We calculated the shielding effect of CDs/PVA films using Fig. 6 b and Eqs. (S1–S8) , and the results are shown in Table S1 . Based on the above data, a histogram of the CDs/PVA blocking efficiency was plotted (Fig. 6 c). The UV-blocking effect of D-CDs/PVA and M-CDs/PVA films can be as high as 98 % or more, and the UV-blocking effect in the HEBL is 85.08 % and 77.16 %, respectively. However, while the visible light transmittance of O-CDs/PVA and P-CDs/PVA films can reach 89.9 % and 76.9 %, respectively, they demonstrate a slightly reduced UV/HEBL blocking effect. The above-mentioned indicates that D-CDs/PVA and M-CDs/PVA have excellent UV absorption performance, which provides some new ideas in the field of blue light protection.

To visualize the UV absorption effect of CDs/PVA polymer films, an organic dye (rhodamine B), which fluoresces orange-red under UV excitation, was used. In Fig. 6 d, the aqueous solution of rhodamine B exhibits bright orange-red fluorescence under 365 nm UV excitation. However, the orange-red fluorescence of the rhodamine B aqueous solution decreased drastically when the CDs/PVA films were placed in front of a 365 nm UV flashlight. The D-CDs/PVA and M-CDs/PVA films effectively restored the original color of the rhodamine B aqueous solution. In contrast, the O-CDs/PVA and P-CDs/PVA films were somewhat less effective at blocking UV light, resulting in the continued emission of orange-red fluorescence from rhodamine B. PVA films doped with 5 wt% D-CDs and M-CDs proved to be sufficient to block all UV rays. This suggests its potential as a novel protective solution for applications such as architectural daylighting (e.g., windows).

Based on the experimental results, a hypothetical mechanism for the ability of CDs/PVA to block UV radiation and HEBL is illustrated in Fig. 7 . CDs exhibit two primary absorption peaks in the ultraviolet spectrum, which are closely associated with their unique chemical structure and the abundance of surface functional groups. During the reaction process, hPEI undergoes a condensation reaction with hydroxybenzaldehyde derivatives, resulting in a series of polymers with varying degrees of polymerization (Fig. 7 with D-CDs). Subsequently, these polymers experience continuous dehydration, crosslinking, and carbonization through Schiff base reactions, ultimately forming a dense carbon core. The π-π* transitions within the carbon core shift to longer wavelengths as the conjugated system expands. The abundant surface groups, such as CN on the carbon core can lead to n-π* transitions. The presence of heteroatoms modifies the distribution of electron clouds and energy states, leading to a maximum UV absorption at a wavelength of 350 nm. Furthermore, as the concentration of CDs increases, aggregation occurs, promoting n-π* electronic transitions, thereby enhancing absorption and extending it into longer wavelengths (the HEBL region).

In the preparation of these four types of CDs, the primary distinction lies in the variations of the hydroxybenzaldehyde derivatives. We hypothesize that steric hindrance primarily influences the degree of conjugation of the CDs during the nucleation process. Consequently, the UV spectra were analyzed in greater detail, and the energy band gap was calculated using the following equation:
( α h[MISNU]) 1/m =B(h[MISNU]-E g )
where, “α” represents the absorption coefficient, “B” is a constant, “hν” denotes the photon energy, and “h” is Planck’s constant, given by h = 4.1356676969 × 10−15 eV·s. The variable “ν” refers to the frequency of the incident photons, while “Eg” indicates the band gap of the semiconductor. For direct band gaps, m = 1/2.

The calculated results are illustrated in Fig. S6 , indicating the following order of band gap sizes: M-CDs (4.70 eV) < D-CDs (5.25 eV) < O-CDs (5.56 eV) = P-CDs (5.56 eV). A smaller band gap indicates a larger conjugated domain in the CDs, and the degree of conjugation further influences the UV absorption range of the CDs. With increasing conjugation, the absorption wavelength shifts toward longer wavelengths, resulting in a redshift.

Upon absorption of UV, CDs undergo continuous molecular vibrations, resulting in electronic transitions from the ground state to the excited state. The excited-state electrons lose stability due to interactions with neighboring atoms and molecules, emitting photons as they return to the ground state — a process known as fluorescence. This mechanism converts UV energy into visible light energy at longer wavelengths, allowing CDs to exhibit both UV absorption and HEBL absorption properties. Based on the previously calculated bandgap and the judgment of CDs on the conjugate domains, it can be inferred that the PL emission peak positions of the four CDs should be in the following order: M-CDs > D-CDs - > O-CDs ≈ P-CDs.

The PL emission spectra of CDs solutions under different excitation conditions are illustrated in Fig. S7 . When excited at the optimal wavelength of 420 nm, D-CDs exhibit a broad emission peak at 542 nm, corresponding to the maximum emission wavelength. As the excitation wavelength varies between 340 nm and 430 nm, the fluorescence emission peak of D-CDs shifts toward longer wavelengths, demonstrating a clear dependence on the excitation wavelength. At an optimal excitation wavelength of 400 nm, O-CDs display a prominent emission peak at 516 nm, corresponding to green fluorescence. Under excitation at 450 nm, M-CDs emit yellow fluorescence, with a peak at 570 nm, while P-CDs emit green fluorescence at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm, peaking at 524 nm. Starting with the size effect of CDs, HR-TEM based on Fig. 2a–d reveals the sizes of the four types of CDs as follows: P-CDs (1.76 nm) < O-CDs (2.84 nm) < M-CDs (2.87 nm) < D-CDs (4.20 nm). As the size of the CDs increases, the energy of the electronic transitions decreases, resulting in a longer emitted fluorescence wavelength.

Furthermore, it is noteworthy that the emission wavelengths of the four types of CDs exhibit a red shift as the excitation wavelength increases. Corresponding to the FT-IR and XPS of Fig. 2f–i, this phenomenon is primarily attributed to the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups (OH) and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms (CN) on the surface of the CDs. These groups and bonds create multiple energy levels at the surface, leading to the fluorescence of carbon dots under varying excitation wavelengths. This fluorescence excitation dependence indicates that the surface defects of the CDs play a dominant role in their luminescence.
The results of this data are interconnected and consistent with the predictions outlined above, thereby supporting our initial hypothesis.

During the preparation of CDs/PVA films, the CN and OH groups of CDs can form hydrogen bonds with the OH groups present in the PVA matrix. This interaction effectively immobilizes UV-absorbing groups, such as the benzene ring and CN, within the CDs/PVA film. Upon absorbing UV energy, molecular vibrations within the film disrupt these hydrogen bonds, allowing the modified film to exhibit both UV absorption and HEBL absorption properties, while also enhancing stability and uniformity. Due to the higher concentration of OH groups in D-CDs, their ability to form hydrogen bonds with PVA is significantly increased, resulting in optimal UV and HEBL absorption properties for D-CDs/PVA. Notably, when the concentration of CDs in the PVA matrix reaches 5 wt%, D-CDs/PVA demonstrates an impressive capability to block 98.87 % of UV radiation and 85.08 % of HEBL.

To investigate the suitability of CDs films as UV and HEBL barrier films, the spectrograms of CDs/PVA in 370 nm and 460 nm blue-light chips were measured, as shown in Fig. 8
a and b. The chip spectrograms show that the pure PVA film shows almost no UV and blue light blocking effect in both the 370 and 460 nm regions, while the UV and HEBL absorption intensities of the lamp beads covered by CDs/PVA are significantly reduced, especially for D-CDs/PVA and M-CDs/PVA.

The spectra and corresponding CIE coordinates of one or two layers of D-CDs/PVA films placed on a self-made cool white LED chip are shown in Fig. 8 c and d. From Fig. 8 c, it is found that the blue-chip had a decreasing emission peak at 400–500 nm as the number of film layers increased, which corresponded to the transmission spectrum of the film, and the hues of the LED shifted from cool-white to warm-white (inset of Fig. 8 c). As illustrated in Fig. 8d, the Correlated Color Temperature (CCT) value decreases from 8138 K to 4501 K and then to 3363 K. A color temperature of approximately 4000 K is considered beneficial for the human eye [38,39]. This decrease is attributed to the contraction of the blue region. The corresponding chromaticity coordinates (x, y) change from (0.2902, 0.3145) to (0.3670, 0.4091) and finally to (0.4287, 0.4091). Similarly, M-CDs showed similar experimental results ( Fig. S8 ). Based on these results, it is confirmed that D-CDs/PVA and M-CDs/PVA can be used for general illumination to block blue light.

In conclusion, four types of CDs were successfully synthesized using a one-step solvothermal method with hPEI and hydroxybenzaldehyde derivatives as precursors. These CDs demonstrated excellent absorption in the UV and HEBL regions, effectively converting the absorbed energy into fluorescence and showcasing remarkable photoconversion efficiency. Furthermore, the CDs exhibited exceptional resistance to both acidic and alkaline conditions, making them suitable for applications in harsh environments. Additionally, CDs/PVA films were created by combining the CDs with PVA, with D-CDs/PVA demonstrating the best performance by blocking over 98.87 % of UV light and up to 85.08 % of HEBL, while maintaining a visible light transmittance of 70 %. Finally, the practical application of the composite films for UV and HEBL shielding was evaluated by laminating the CDs/PVA onto LED chips. In summary, D-CDs offer an innovative solution for UV absorption and blue light protection.

Wanting Li: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xilang Jin: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Data curation. Xuehao Ma: Methodology, Data curation. Bin Huang: Software, Formal analysis. Xinyu Zhang: Validation. Xiao Li: Methodology. Qingfa Si: Project administration. Weixing Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Technology Innovation Leading Program of Shaanxi (No. 2020QFY07-05) and the Natural Science Foundation Research Project of Shaanxi Province (No. 2023-JC-YB-087).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2025.117978.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
